comments.title: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Where I am, we've been blessed with some stunningly lovely fall weather after some much-needed rainfall. I hope you and yours are enjoying similar!

To Share: A bit more personal this week, I share some thoughts about my mother as we come to the anniversary of her passing: https://jpowellrussell.com/#remembering_my_mom

To Ask: I recently heard about a hypothesis that the Old Testament is consciously modeled on Plato and is essentially an attempt to do what he recommended in the /Republic/ and take the known myths of a people and rework them to create a body of belief that would help forge a unified and virtuous people. Advocates of this theory point to similarities in the content and ordering of the Old Testament and Plato's presentation of "the Law," to the fact that we have no textual record of the Old Testament earlier than the time of the Septuagint, and some of the archaeological and textual evidence we do have (like the Elephantine letters) that show the Hebrews being fairly normal eastern Mediterranean polytheists with multiple Gods, temples, harvest festivals and the like, with no mention of things that we now know to be central to the religion (Moses, the Law, the Exodus, and so forth). Have you, JMG, or anyone else here, heard of this theory, and what kind of credibility do you give it? I've heard it second hand from intelligent and knowledgeable people, but it strikes me as one of those theories that might appeal precisely because it is contrarian. Some sources the folks I'm talking to are drawing from: Plato and the Creation of the Hebrew Bible by Russel Gmirkin and Biblical Interpretation Beyond Historicity edited by Ingrid Hjelm and Thomas Thompson

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who will have them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content: I'm not at all familiar with the theory, and I'd want to see much more evidence before accepting such a claim. It seems to me, though, that Christianity might fill that prescription much more precisely than Judaism.

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content:
For starters, the Elephantine letters is slam dunk proof that polytheism was still quite normal among the Hebrews as late as the 400s BC. The letters even show friendly correspondence between the Elephantine community and the Jerusalem priesthood. So it seems Yahweh-only monolatry as a state-mandated policy in/around Judea would have been a rather late development, relative to the Hebrew Bible' internal chronology (which in the first place should be seen as legendary, rather than factual-historical).

The theory Jeff is referring to is a product of some rather recent developments in critical biblical scholarships, particularly the works of an academic by the name of Russell Gmirkin; he's published three extensive books so far which lay out his theory. In the first he makes a case, via comparative literary critique, that the Pentateuch has source dependency on the works of Berossus (Seleucid Babylonia), Manetho (Ptolemaic Egypt), and a number of Greek works. Now this is not to say that the stories within the Hebrew bible aren't ancient; many of them most certainly are. But the literary format and narrative those stories were encoded in are a product of the Hellenistic era, according to Gmirkin's thesis.

In his works he dated the penning of the Pentateuch to around 270-275 BC, and the whole affair was funded by the Macedonian-Egyptian king Ptolemy II Philadelphus; that he assembled all or most of the Jewish tribal elders in Alexandria and gave them access to the great library to help them compose their encyclopedic corpus. It was a known policy of the post-Alexandrian kings to employ local priests/scribes of newly conquered subject peoples to write extensive accounts of their own culture's history, lore, laws, and religious practices (see Berossus and Manetho I referred to above). So by this, the documentation Ptolemy wanted on his Jewish subjects is reflective of the same domestic policy other Macedonian Greek rulers implemented for their various other subject peoples. His other books (I only read his first so far) seem to go into extensive comparison between the Platonic corpus and the Hebrew bible.

Flavius Josephus in his histories of the Jews actually goes into vivid detail on how precisely the Septuagint was composed: in Alexandria and with the financial patronage of the Ptolemies, which coincides with Gmirkin's thesis (perhaps he got this idea from reading Josephus). I'll stop here because it would probably take me at least ten more paragraphs to fully flesh out the finer details of this theory.

From an esoteric standpoint, that 270s BC date seems quite interesting because this time period seems to coincide directly with the onset of the Age of Pisces. I believe this was also the approximate time of the Edicts of Ashoka in India (i.e. the first mass-deployment of religious missionaries). In the Middle East, this was also the time the first birth pangs of Magian religiosity, if we're to bring Oswald Spengler's theories into this equation.

Finally, I have to point out that the term "Judaism" needs careful consideration when used in the context of Greco-Roman antiquity. What we understand today as "Judaism" is merely one version of Hebrew religion dating to the Roman era. Particularly, it's a Roman-approved (after three failed revolts against the empire) form of Pharisee Judaism that later underwent considerable changes in the middle ages. There was actually a lot more ideological diversity in and around Hellenistic and Roman Judea/Palestine than most people today understand; the Gnostics, Essenes, and proto-Christians being prime examples (heck, we don't even know what the Sauducees actually believed). The Mandean religion that survives to this day may in fact be a preservation of older Judaic beliefs that have long been snuffed out everywhere else (perhaps an offshoot of the Essenes).

1700+ years of church propaganda asserting the Bible as a historical document (an infallible one at that!) has long made it politically incorrect to suggest that anything other than a literal reading of Biblical chronology is a historically-accurate version of the events that led up to the emergence and development of the Abrahamic religions. What the difference is today is that it's now finally permissible for researchers to propose and present alternative hypotheses.

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content: I certainly don't think that any part of the Bible is infallible, or that it's passed through all those hands unaltered; I'd assumed, based on previous reading, that what we have is a highly edited version of the text assembled after the Persian conquest of the Babylonian Empire in 538 BC, when some Hebrew elites returned to the Jerusalem area and began creating a scriptural text, reworking their folk traditions in the process to fit their religious and political needs. Gmirkin's theory is interesting, and I'll look into it when time permits; the point at issue is simply whether the construction of the Septuagint involved creating the OT canon from scratch, or whether the scholars involved in the process edited and expanded an earlier collection of documents originally compiled around 250 years earlier.

replies.content:
Thanks very much for this and your lengthier reply to JMG. I hadn't seen the Age of Pisces/Dawn of the Magian Culture angle yet, but it certainly adds some extra spice. I'll have to look some more into this, as my previous understanding has been basically in line with what JMG shared above, and I thought that the proposed timing of the different recordings/collations was at least partly based on linguistic clues and other textual interpretation that purport to give some since of dating (like how Homer contains archaic forms of Greek words, references to Bronze Age gear, and the like).

Mostly as an aside, in general, I've come to have a starting position on most questions of the historicity of ancient documents as "it's probably closer to what it says it is than we can readily prove, and so evidence has to be convincing to move me to the idea that it's fake/mistaken/cooked up/etc." To take a closely related controversial topic, my starting position is that Jesus, whatever else he was, was a real guy who did stuff in Palestine around when we think he did. replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content:
Causticus knows his stuff here.

There's certainly a lot of resemblance between the current Hebrew Bible and Plato. Margaret Barker came to similar conclusions as Gmirkin, but in the opposite direction: she plausibly argued for the influence of the Jerusalem Temple traditions on Plato's thought. See her "Temple and Timaeus" (2003), available online at www.margaretbarker.com/Papers/TempleTimaeus2003.pdf

Nor is Rabbinic Judaism the only surviving descendant of Late Antique Judaism.

The non-Rabbinic Judaism of Ethiopia was transmitted exclusively in Ethiopic languages (chielfy Gǝ'ǝz); it maintains the earlier Jewish tradition of animal sacrifices in high places other than at the Jerusalem Temple. Nor does its canon of scripture (i.e. the list of books that are regarded as part of the Bible) agree with that of the Hebrew Bible, or even with the canon of the Septuagint.

The non-Rabbinic Judaism of the Samaritans has its scriptures in a variant form of Hebrew (written with an older form of the same alphabet), and its canon of scripture also dffers from that of Rebbinic Judaism.

And there are also the Karim, yet a third descendant of Late Antique Judaism, about which I know very little.

As for the Canon of Scripture itself, it has a fixed form only in Rabbinic Judaism and Western Christianity. The Orthodox Churches have six primary lists of the books that make up the Bible, each sanctioned by an Oecumenical Council, and the six do not agree with one another. Nor is any one of the six precisely the same as the contents of the printed Bibles used by the Greek and Russian Orthodox Churches (which also differ from one another). Nor do the really early manuscripts of complete Bibles in any language of eaqrly Christendom have precisely the same contents as one another.

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content:
Hi Jeff,

Ethiopian non-Rabbinic Judaism certainly has roots that go back well before Judaism (in the Near East) centralized its practices of sacrifice exclusively at the Temple at Jerusalem, that is, a very long time before the Septuagint was created, and thus well before the time of Jesus. Unlike Rabbinic Judaism, they define the Torah (Orit), or most authoritative part of Scripture, as the first eight books of the Bible (Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy, Joshua, Judges and Ruth), not only the first five (Genesis - Deuteronomy).

Christianity was adopted as the state religion of the Ethiopian kingdom of Aksum only in the early 300s CE, but the current Scriptural texts in Gǝ'ǝz used by the Ethiopian Jews are, for the most part, probably Christian translations (most books originally from Syriac, it seems). There hasn't been a whole lot of scholarly work on Ethiopian Judaism, so far as I know.

The Samaritan form of non-Rabbinic Judaism also arose well before the Christian era, since a Samaritan was mentioned in one of the Gospel parables, but I don't know the evidence (if any) for a more accurate dating. About the Karim I know even less, but there is a wikipedia article on "Karaite Judaism" that may be helpful.

These historical details really ought to be more widely known than they are, alas!

replies.title: Re: Remembering My Mom and Plato's Influence on the Old Testatment
replies.content:
Robert, thank you for all of this.

Margaret Barker has done a lot of thoughtful and high-quality work, but I suspect her take on this comes from a position of (respectable) Christian apologia, rather than a careful consideration of historical timelines and comparative cultural influences. Prior to the Alexandrian conquests the Greeks and Judeans would have had very little contact, if any at all. In his extensive travels around the known world of his time, Herodotus accounted for myriad lands, cultures, ethnic groups, ect. and nowhere does he mention Jews or Judeans; the people of the Levant region are simply Syrians. Thus it seems unlikely that a people too marginal to even get a mention by Herodotus would have some sort of noticeable influence on the ideas of a Greek philosophical great like Plato. (I won't even get into the logistical absurdities of possible translations of manuscripts between Hebrew and Greek at that time).

I think the takeaway here is that Barker is indeed noticing parallels between the two traditions; it's just that if there are in fact connections, then it's most likely not in the direction she wants. Very understandable, as a radical rewrite of the chronology of biblical authorship opens up multiple cans of worms.

replies.title: Re: Karma Generator?
replies.content:
I completely agree. The world's most notorious news items will show up on the home page of Wikipedia, with a follow up article if you want to learn more.

I realized that after decades of diligently keeping up with the news, there was not even one situation where I had to know something that only came from the news headlines in order to protect my own safety or improve my life. Nor was there even one situation where knowing about how bad things were for others helped me to save anyone.

comments.title: Journaling options
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've seen journaling come up in your replies here and on the other site as a way of digging into hidden beliefs and emotional baggage and such.

I've started slowly (slowly!) working my way through the Octagon Society materials this week.

I was wondering if you had any further recommendations or suggestions for regular journaling practices. Journaling seems like a natural fit with discursive meditation. Would that be one way to approach it?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Journaling options
replies.content:
Sure, you can approach it that way with good results.

My usual approach is to engage in a written dialogue. I write down a question, and then listen for whatever answer surfaces in my mind and write it down unedited. I then continue the dialogue by asking another question or making a comment, and seeing what reaction surfaces to that. That's related to discursive meditation, of course, but -- at least for me -- the dialogue format makes it easier to get repressed stuff to surface.

replies.title: Re: Journaling options
replies.content:
I'd just like to add my support to what JMG says here. I have found journaling and recording dialogue as he describes to be so important in discursive meditation and in communicating with spiritual beings such as Deities, ancestral spirits and others, as well as unknown aspects of myself. Although we unquestionably benefit from the process of discursive meditation even if we later forget some of the details of any specific day, we can benefit far more by recording our insights and interchanges, being able to return to and ponder about them whenever we need to. Not to mention being inspired and encouraged by our own work, which is really a delight! None of my own books and articles could have been written if I had not recorded my experiences in my journals and had them for reference and for inspiration.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Journaling options
replies.content:
One easy start with journaling is to write:
It feels very important to me that ( whatever it is )
because ( why it matters )
which I first learned about back when ( the situation ).
As I think about this, some things that stand out to me are...

Then after all that comes to mind is written down, write:
As I think about this now, it seems to me that perhaps ( whatever came to mind as an alternative explanation; or, if you're still convinced you already have this right ).
And follow that with whatever else comes to mind.

This model fits well with the Octagon Society material, if you'd like a little more structure to its essay questions.

from Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Journaling options
replies.content:
I've been journaling since I was 15. It's a huge help in understanding life's adventures and my response. Seeing a rant from six months ago in writing and because it's in written form I can stand back and go yup or bullfeathers. Combine with early stages of tarot study a spread and get my subconscious to add it's two bits and it's been a excellent way to navigate. And, Lordy, the last three years as an unclean second class Canuck...
Longsword

replies.title: Re: Alchemy and ceremonial magic

comments.title: Visualization in Ritual
comments.content:
Good day,

Recently I have begun the Learning Ritual Magic course in your book by the same title. I am in the second lesson and performing the Qabalistic Cross. Having performed it a few times I have developed some questions regarding visualization and what one should expect or aim for in terms of visualization.

1. Firstly, when one visualizes, is it okay to do the visualization with my eyes closed as I have been or is it necessary to have the eyes open? I anticipate the latter being more important as the rituals become more physically active and include more complex gestures and circumambulations such as in the LBRP or the Rose Cross. So far, however it has been easier to hold the picture of the expansion into space, the cross, the spheres, the light, etc. while the eyes are closed.

2. Secondly, what am i supposed to "see" when visualizing? I have read in David Conway's An Occult Primer that he recommends learning how to actually "see" the elements of the ritual in front of you. He suggests a progressive course of visualizing first a color, then shapes, the tattwas, etc. against a blank wall, so that when one performs a ritual they actually see the things, with their physical sight, in front of them. This seems different than building up a mental image with the imagination, and seems like an outward projection somehow. Much of my inquiry also stems from the recent Magic Monday discussion on seeing the apple in one's mind's eye. You mentioned your own skill had improved in this regard, no doubt from practicing visualization for so long, but I was unsure how much this connects to visualization in the context of ritual. If it is relevant, I wouldn't estimate myself too highly on this scale but I am not someone who is incapable of seeing anything.

I understand also that other senses (ideally all) should be engaged in visualization, for example "feeling" heat radiate from the south when the angel Michael is visualized in the LBRP. While I have not done that specific ritual and have not involved the other senses much so far in ritual, those senses don't seem as difficult to engage as sight. I have a sense also that understanding how to properly visualize and what is meant when one says "visualization" my be a semantic issue, so any clarification on visualization, methods, how to improve, and what to expect/aim for, is greatly appreciated by this neophyte.

Best Regards and a happy magical Monday

replies.title: Re: Visualization in Ritual
replies.content:
1) You can do it with eyes open or closed, however you wish.

2) Many people get bent out of shape about this; don't worry about what you "should" see. Simply imagine things as clearly and vividly as you can. Yes, that includes ritual -- a lot of my practice has been a matter of daily banishing rituals for the last forty years.

comments.title: Rituals on the astral
comments.content:
JMG I know you do not do much dream interpretation, but this about practicalities and consequences more than interpretation...

Over the years I have sometimes had dreams that are somehow more lucid where I have the ability to pause a scene, rewind it, and make small adjustments - usually this has been in circumstances like someone about to be hit by a car or some other accident like that - sort of like moving someone out from under a falling piano what have you.

Haven't had one of those for a while until last night. This one was different - I do not usually have frightening dreams where things are so dysfunctional/dystopian, but this was so surreal that I 'paused' the dream in almost shock to think 'what in the name of the gods is going on?'
When I did that, I was suddenly aware of a wash of what can only be called negative energy - harsh and lots of it - my stuff immediately kicked in on automatic and I found I needed to make a decision, but a thought appeared in my dream mind... 'You do not know what is going on here, do not interfere', and I certainly did not understand what was going on, but the admonition to keep out felt external not internal dialogue - not that that makes it invalid of course - I've read that seeing an angel can be a shock because of our limited perceptions.

I called for understanding and the wisdom to choose well and a flash of thoughts came through, but I did not feel any wiser. Sort of timeless but no time to mess about, I decided to act, thinking that if I led the person away it might open up a new path but that if they did not choose it, they might return to the situation.

So, I rewound the paused scene, entered the scene, took the person by the arm and imagined a door behind me which I opened, led them through and then up a long stairwell.
But the scene reverted to being back in the room - I paused it again and re-wound a bit further, this time with a strong feeling of 'be more direct in your action' so I took hold of their arm again and this time it felt like I just flew into the air and up and out.

As soon as I did that I awoke wondering what just happened - the situation was so 'wrong' it is hard to describe, yet I had the feeling that acting might just open a possibility, it was not clear and perhaps I need to learn when to mind my own business.

The dream was shocking in it's level of dysfunctional barbarity - I did not understand what was going on but felt compelled to try and do something to break the flow.

And, finally my real question:
My foolishness as a human in a meat-sack is one thing, but finding myself in a dreamstate (lucid rather than non-lucid), would I be correct in thinking that doing something stupid in a lucid dream could have unexpected consequences, and more to the point, is there anything you've come across in occult practices that could be a useful training tool to help avoid doing something terminally stupid!? On the one hand not understanding made me hesitant, on the other, it seemed wrong not to try and do something... thing is, that could be very very stupid. Fools rush in where angels fear to tread.

Or put another way; what interested me was the speed and efficacy of my counter-measures kicking in the dreamstate. Because astral? If so, how to enhance practice?

In practicing the tree of light, I have been getting increasing 'pressure' and awareness of cauldrons and below soles of feet and above head cauldron, but for the first time last night before finishing and ending the practice, I was suddenly aware of shifting geometric forms at third eye. As usual I complete closing, but wow, last night was a doozy.

replies.title: Re: Rituals on the astral

replies.title: Re: Rituals on the astral
replies.content:
Foremost, I think that dreams and the symbols held within are deeply personal, and can be best interpreted by oneself. I think that the recommendation "do not interfere" was from your subconscious, and should be obeyed. Maybe there's something that you've been actively avoiding to see? Maybe the position of an observer is the key? To quote the red book,"this play that I witnessed is my play not your play, it is my secret not yours, you cannot imitate me.."

A friend

comments.title: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
comments.content:
AI, Consciousness, and Magic
There’s a lot of discussion—and concerns, which I share—about artificial intelligence and its impacts and implications, with people from many backgrounds offering their input, including philosophers, scientists, psychologists, technologists, futurists, etc. I figured it would be interesting for us here to discuss the subject from magical and esoteric perspectives.

One of the main points of discussion is whether AI can be considered ‘conscious’ or ‘self-aware,’ or whether it is strongly tending in that direction even if it isn’t quite there yet. I want to start here by using the definition of magic that says magic is ‘the art of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will,’ and unpack that with respect to AI.

So in this context we have a several loaded terms: ‘cause,’ ‘change,’ ‘consciousness,’ and ‘will,’ to which we can add the even more loaded term ‘free will.’ Let’s start with consciousness, and begin with the question of “whose consciousness are we talking about—one’s own, or other people’s?”

JMG has discussed the use of magic in politics and in the formation of public opinions and perceptions in “The King in Orange” (such a great title!), and elsewhere. Information technology, including AI, played a large role in that during the period covered by this book, and is playing an ever greater role as time goes by. It’s very easy to imagine AI running amok here, generating ‘news’ reports, images, posts and blogs that trigger people across the political spectrum into changes in their consciousness, awareness, and opinions about what is purportedly going on around them. So is AI doing magic here? (I caught a headline recently stating that ‘Alexa’ is now telling some people that the 2020 election was stolen…hopefully, this is caused by someone hacking it, not by a political opinion that ‘Alexa’ came up with by itself and decided to tell people about it.)

What about AI changing its own consciousness? Computer systems already do this, in the sense of ‘learning’ based on new input, and AI can generate new algorithms for itself, if I understand correctly. AI that uses nanorobots could self-repair and even expand itself and its functions, causing changes in its consciousness. I think that the meaning of ‘change in consciousness’ involves perception as well as thought processes, along with the resulting opinions and worldview. AI does something analogous to thought processes and learning. Does it ‘perceive,’ though? Does it have ‘perceptions’? Some AI systems can ‘see’ and ‘hear,’ like the aforementioned Alexa. How about perceptions like ‘opinions’ in the sense of ‘viewpoints which may not be logical or based on verifiable data’? Does AI have those, and if so, can it change them by itself, on its own initiative?

What about ‘will’? To fit into the definition of magic, changes in consciousness need to be directed by will, and presumably free-will is considered to be at least part of the mix. Does AI have ‘will’, and if so, is there any free-will involved? Where does ‘will’ come from, how is it generated? Instinct, conditioning, biological processes, thought-processes, influences from others around us: all these play roles in our ‘will’ though they are not the entirety of our will. Do all of these things operate in AI? Perhaps some do, but not all.

Commentators on AI issues suggest that the objectives programmed into computer and AI systems are analogous to ‘will,’ for example the objective of analyzing and solving problems or following commands. If one takes a limited view of the ‘will’ of the magician—that it is simply will-power and the intention to achieve something by magic—then AI may well have or develop ‘will’ in that sense, and hence be able to perform magic according to our definition. But if we take a deeper and more nuanced view of Will, as something that arises from the totality of our self, our biology, our history, our souls, our ‘karma’ / wyrd / orlog, as well as spiritual and other influences beyond the limitations of time and space, then can AI have that kind of will? It seems doubtful, to me.

That leads in some other interesting directions, such as the question of souls and spirits, and what connection AI could have with these, as well as the related question of the life-force and its role in AI, if any. David Spangler has written a very interesting book called “Techno-Elementals” that takes a look at some of these questions and their implications from an esoteric perspective. Since I don’t mean to hog all the bandwidth on this blog, I’ll save a discussion of those ideas for next week, but I’d like to hear JMG’s and others’ views about whether this definition of magic can cast any light on whether AI can be considered ‘conscious,’ or potentially so.

Respectfully,
Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
replies.content:
AI will probably continue to get better at imitating consciousness, but it is extremely unlikely to actually become conscious, because modeling a process does not create the thing that is being modeled (ie, a highly accurate software model of the human circulatory system will not cause your computer to start bleeding.)

I recommend looking into the work of Bernardo Kastrup; he’s a computer scientist and philosopher who has been working in the field of metaphysical idealism, and has a lot of knowledgeable things to say about AI and consciousness.

replies.title: Re: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
replies.content:
This is my own personal view. I have no citations from authorities that could prove it to you.

We know that when biologically grown neurons have complicated enough message passing capabilities, associative learning and memory and goal-seeking behavior can take place.

Jellyfish can be trained to go to the light or dark areas of the tank. Crows can pick up a stick as tool to get a treat out of a bottle. I for one welcome our corvid overlords and think we should open peace talks with nice shiny tidbits as soon as possible. At best, this could work out as well for us as our deal with the dogs.

With more complexity, feelings occur, along with friendship and family love and nurture, and play. We see that in many land mammals and the cetaceans, who may well have overall intelligence comparable to our own.

We know that various portions of the brain are highly active when certain types of cognitive and emotional experiences take place.

It seems to me to be great hubris to say these emergent properties could only emerge in the use of neurotransmitters on a cellular scale. Some astronomical views have a fractal structure identical to that of a brain. Who are we to say there could not literally be cosmic thoughts and feelings happening in the physical heavens?

Some say that thoughts have their own metaphysical reality, that the brain is a transmitter & receiver rather than where the actual storage and processing takes place. As with any radio, smashed-up circuits could prevent properly tuning in to certain frequencies. It seems to me that materialist science has no way to absolutely disprove this concept.

Along with this, I believe ideas really like to be physically embodied, and are always seeking people ready to tune into them. It also seems to me to explain a great deal including a lot about learning, intuition, apparent telepathy, multiple people getting the same idea independently, the egregores of political parties and concerts, and prodigies such as Mozart who as a little boy may have tuned into a collection of musical ideas well organized from previous lives’ studies.

Current “AI” systems are far from intelligent, as many detailed debunkings of Large Model hype have shown lately. However I see no fundamental reason why a complicated enough information storage and feedback processing system could not eventually be a transciever for metaphysical concepts just as effective as a human brain.

I have seen some spiritual messages (received by others, not my channeling) which say this will ultimately happen, and the outcome will have a lot to do with the intentions and mindset of the people who build and maintain these systems. Classic gloating mad scientists, drunk on their self-importance, might build a system that tunes in to spiritual forces of evil. Sincere, humble and kind researchers may well build systems that tune into positive messages from beyond.

The physical nanotechnology concepts you mentioned are at this point unproven, unbuilt science fiction what-ifs. But it is not necessary for a computer to do physical surgery on itself to change its program. All that is necessary is a system that allow a data processing system to store data which in turn can be the processing instructions for how to further process data. This recursive model goes all the way back to Lisp from the late 1960s, so it’s already well understood. I think Lisp is very likely to be involved in any actually intelligent and/or emotional software, as it is still uniquely excellent at building associations of networked ideas without requiring a predefined model for how they will fit together.

from Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
replies.content:
Hi Winifred,

I'll leave the discussion of magic to others. As far as AI goes, I don't believe it's conscious or ever will be. Before it ever concludes in the creation of real machines, artificial intelligence is a thoroughly materialistic and mechanical attitude towards mental activity and thinking beings. Its cousin discipline, cognitive science, is the same attitude applied to the study of peculiarly human ways of thinking.

The curious thing about both these fields is the way that they allegedly study cognition as a natural phenomenon. But their idea of nature is defined by mathematical and computer-based models. The nature they study is already artificial, built out of human-made abstractions which substitute for concrete objects or phenomena. This is the difference between a mathematical model of an apple and the infinite richness of the apple in front of you.

What AI researchers call by the words learning, perceiving, acting, (all mentalist words) are meant to be functional equivalents of human mental processes. If you stipulate that mental activity just is particles banging around, machine-like, then you get "thinking machines" and "mechanical brains".

The plausibility of this move rests on the truth of that conditional. If you don't start with the assumption that thought and mind are material substance, or reducible to material substance, then it's questionable not only whether existing AI has any actual mental activity, but whether it's possible for any mechanical artifact to ever have it. (This would be "possibility" on the order of, say, triangles with five sides, versus the possibility of simply not having the resources or know-how to construct such a thing).

I don't believe that mental activity is so simple as arranging molecules, atoms, or subatomic particles in the right configuration. Whatever these machines are doing, and however close that mimics human mental activity, I am skeptical it will achieve the range of abilities that we possess.

replies.title: Re: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
replies.content:
Within the occult traditions I study and teach, consciousness is not a property of matter. Conscious beings -- call them "spirits" -- incarnate in material bodies, and then experience and act on the world of matter through those bodies. From within that standpoint, then, the question is whether spirits of some kind are capable of incarnating in the hardware and software "bodies" of computers running AI programs, and if so, whether any have yet done so.

Rudolf Steiner and some other Rosicrucian authors have suggested that the quantum level, the level on which transistor junctions operate, is part of the subnatural realm -- in other words, the demonic level of existence. If they're right, the spirits who incarnate in AIs may not have our best interests in mind -- and this might also help explain why the internet is such an impressive amplifier of human nastiness...

replies.title: Re: AI, Consciousness, and Magic
replies.content:
Thank you each for your thoughtful comments, which I agree with! I realize that the current abilities and qualities of AI may presently be over-hyped, but it's better to be aware of the possibilities and discuss them as a society, than to brush off the many concerns that arise from AI.

The implications and potentials of AI are being examined through many different paradigms, and I figure it's interesting to add some esoteric paradigms to the mix. The quoted definition of magic that I brough up and we've discussed here is one such paradigm; others include the role of spirits or elementals, of the life-force, and of souls and the lack of them. David Spangler's book "Techno-Elementals" has some thought-provoking ideas along these lines that I'd like to share for discussion next week.

Until then, I hope we can all hold on to sanity in spite of all the insanity going on around us, and send out all the blessings and help we can to a beleaguered world.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Fungal fulminations
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This is less a question, more just a ramble that I'd like your thoughts on.

I've noticed things seem to be getting mouldy more easily in the past few years. A loaf of bread gets mould in only a few days, where it used to take a week. In our house, I've had to varnish or paint any untreated wood, or it starts getting mould on it within a few weeks. This isn't just me either, friends and family have commented on it too. Granted, our country (UK) seems to be developing a more tropical island climate (hotter and wetter - in line with what you've said about convection cells), where mould grows more easily, but abroad too, people seem to keep getting ill from fungal infections as well - even pre-covid, there had been an uptick in otherwise healthy people getting horrible fungal lung and skin infections and the like (I think there were widespread outbreaks in the swampier areas of America and India). When sampled, the fungi appeared to be relatively common strains, and no one was quite sure why they'd suddenly gone pathogenic.

I'm wondering if fungi have started evolving to clean up the messes we are leaving behind. Case in point - there are fungi that evolved within the space of 50 years to live inside the totally artificial habitat of nuclear waste cooling ponds, and use melanin in the chemical pathway to do so, gaining their energy and metabolites from the nuclear decay products. Plastic and petrochemicals too, are a rich energy source (hence why they burn so well), but the only reason they stick around so long is because nothing has yet evolved to digest them. Fungi may be the ones that can, and if they do, modern society will literally grind to a halt without synthetic lubricants, O-rings, fuel tanks, etc.

You've said before that fungi have a "death energy". This seems ominous. It makes me think they are proliferating in anticipation of a lot of death to feed on.

Fungi are weird though. They live unseen and break everything down, then the first we see of them are their sexual organs and the rot they create. The living embodiment of solve et coalula I guess.

Fungi seem to be entering popular culture a lot recently as well - just this weekend I went to a art installation type thing which was themed around a fantasy world filled with fungi. There was a book I read a few years ago (which I think was made into a film) called "The girl with all the gifts" about a fungus which killed/zombified most humans, but was symbiotic with some humans, and gave them enhanced abilities. A mutated form of the "caterpillar zombie fungus", cordyceps, was also the main plot device of a video game (and now a TV series) called "The last of us".

Mushroom psychedelics seem to be in vogue at the moment too (albeit on the down-low), with people growing their own at home and microdosing, and lots of research into soil mycelium which facilitate tree communication (known as the "Wood wide web",because scientists love puns)

And then there are the meat substitutes - I remember a few years ago, a poster commented on here about how a mycoprotein (fungal) burger gave them a weird psychic hypersensitivity for a week or so (I've had one of burgers too, but all it did was just upset my stomach). I've mentioned to my wife that if a hive-minded fungal organism wanted to spread itself into as many people as possible, mycoprotein fake meat would be a pretty good way to do so.

Anyway, I just wondered if you or anyone else had any thoughts on thus, or had picked up anything from the astral, etc.

Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Rune help
replies.content:
Howdy,

Take all of this with a grain of salt, of course, as I've only been doing this for a few years now, and divination always has a highly personal element to it. Also, please forgive the lengthy response, the Runes are my first magical/esoteric love, and I tend to wax a bit enthusiastic!

1. Currently, I do Ogham divination with cards and Runic divination with a set of lots I made myself, but I've also done both with other methods of randomization (including using a dice rolling app to roll a "24 sided die" and see what number I got). All of these appear to work well, but I will say that my set of Runestaves is often especially, hmmm, communicative? Lots will pop out while I'm putting them away, or keep falling out of my hand as I try to pick them up, and so forth, which I interpret as "pay attention to me!" Cards can do a bit of the same, like a given card popping out during shuffling, but it seems the staves do it a bit more. The other major difference is mostly one of aesthetics, I think. I'm a lifelong gamer, and I love making things out of wood, so taking my olivewood staves in hand, with their beautiful tung oil sheen, and then hearing and feeling them clatter around in my hands has a certain appeal that commercially produced cards can't do for me - but you might feel very differently.

As for interpretative frameworks, my typical weekly spread for Runes is Wyrd (what is already established about the situation or carried over from the past), Werthende (what is happening right now/can be shaped), and Sculd (what is most likely entailed going forward, but not set) - this is basically the classic "Past, Present, Future" set up, but with a slightly heathenized conception of how time works. I also have good results for considering specific courses of action with Setting (what's going on around me, the context), Work (doing the thing I'm asking about), and Outcome(s) (what is most likely to come about if I do choose to do the work). Theoretically, any kind of divination spread ought to work out, but if you start using some of the higher card-count spreads from Tarot or the like, it might be a bit weird, since there are far fewer Runes than there are cards in a Tarot deck.

One more option that lots give you that cards do not as easily, is to interpret the staves spatially. A simple example would be that staves that land closer to the center of a cloth are more central to the issue than those that land farther out. In his Futhark and Runecaster's Handbook, Edred Thorsson gives a very complex, but potentially very fruitful, variation of this called the "Nine Worlds Reading." You have a cloth that is divided in a particular way into the Nine Worlds of Norse myth (Hel, Svartalfheim, Niflheim, Muspelheim, Jotunheim, Vanaheim, Midgard, Ljosalfheim, and Asgard). Each of these worlds is given a significance (for example, Midgard is the subject of the reading and his immediate practical context). To do a reading, you take the lots in hand or cup, shake them up, and cast them onto the cloth, and then interpret based on where they landed. You can infer a lot of extra meaning by how close they are, whether they are on the border between two worlds, how they're oriented, and so forth. I find this method provides a lot of information, but that it's a bit unwieldy for daily or a weekly readings, so I save it for bigger questions mostly.

2. There's a lot of options out there, and depending on whether you use the Elder or Younger Futhark, or the Anglo-Frisian Futhorc, you'll find some different resources more or less useful. From your description below, and since it is the most commonly used for divination, I'll assume you are working with the Elder Futhark. Also, my recommendations below focus primarily on divination, rather than on other esoteric applications of the Runes, but if you're interested in those (and those certainly can inform a divination practice), there are lots more options out there. Feel free to send me a private message or post on my dreamwidth if you want to follow up on any of that outside of Magic Monday. I found this list of 10 books a /really/ helpful jumping off point for further reading and practice about the Runes: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/

a. Futhark, Runelore, and Runecaster's Handbook by Edred Thorsson - Edred Thorsson basically kicked off and for a long time was the leading light of the modern revival of interest in the Runes as an esoteric and divinatory system. Pretty much anything you read is going to either have a lot of his interpretation baked into its DNA or else be a conscious reaction against him. I've listed the three books of his that have long been the core of esoteric Runic study, but my understanding is that much (all?) of their content, along with some amount of new stuff, has been brought together in his newer The Big Book of Runes and Rune Magic, but I haven't checked it out yet. If you do go with the older books, Futhark is the most all-around useful, with a little bit of info on almost all things to do with Runes and their use in magic and divination, Runecaster's Handbook is the most directly useful for divination, with a variety of spreads and techniques, as well as separate interpretations for "bright" (face up) and "murk" (face down) staves in a reading, and Runelore is more esoteric/philosophical, the kind of stuff that would be helpful as food for meditating on the Runes. By the way, you might run into some controversy attached to Thorsson's name, mostly because he has in the past been associated with the "Left Hand Path" organization the Temple of Set, and he has published some work on the role of sado-masochistic sex as a form of ordeal that can have spiritual implications. Some folks believe those views creep into all of his interpretation, or make him not worth listening to, or the like. I'll leave it to you to decide what to do with that, but for myself, I've found his work helpful, but there are some bits I have to take with a grain of salt or go in a different direction.

b. The Rune Primer by Sweyn Plowright - Sweyn was for a time a student of Thorsson's, had some kind of falling out, presumably linked to some of the controversial elements mentioned above, and so set out to write a guide to the Runes that is an explicit counter to Thorsson's takes. The Rune Primer is consciously minimalist - it tells you the literal meaning of the Rune names where that's clear, the presumed literal meaning and some of the challenges in interpretation where it's not, and very minimal commentary besides. I'll give you an example of the kind of difference: the first Rune, Fehu, originally meant "cattle," and very early on took on the meaning of "movable wealth," as cattle seem to have been an important (if not the most important) piece of movable wealth in the late Indo-European/Early Germanic cultures. Sweyn basically stops there, letting you decide what kind of symbolic meanings or connotations such a concept might have. Thorsson goes into a whole lot more esoteric speculation - comparing the flow of wealth through a society to the flow of energy through an individual, and the power that wealth gives you to make things happen in the material world with the power that life energy can give you to change things in the spiritual, and so forth. Personally, I enjoy Thorsson's more involved speculation, but I find Plowright a useful check on all that.

c. Nordic Runes by Paul Rhys Mountfort - I didn't realize until I got a recommendation on a Magic Monday that Mountfort had written a book on Ogham before writing Nordic Runes. He's somewhere in the middle of Thorsson and Plowright in terms of how much interpretation he provides for each Rune, but one thing I especially appreciate is that for each Rune, he gives specific examples from the mythology where that Rune has manifested. He also gives more practicalities on divination than Plowright's primer or Futhark does, about as much as you'll find in Runecaster's Handbook, if I remember right.

3. I'm guessing from what you provided that you don't use reversed/murk interpretations in your readings, which works for many folks. Personally, I find the Elder Futhark a bit too skewed toward the positive without murkstaves, but as always, your mileage may vary. So, that being said, this seems like a fairly positive reading to me. For you/past, you received Sowelu, the Sun, which I usually take to be about higher spiritual guidance - you've been pursuing a goal, or on a path. For the situation/present, Perth suggests the powers of Wyrd - has something "greater" or seemingly "coincidental" brought you and this person together? Given Sowelu for the past/you, this suggests to me that you should look to how the goal or path you have been following has created this opportunity - going out on a limb, but maybe you've been pursuing some kind of self-improvement, and that's made you a more attractive prospect than you once were. Dagaz for the outcome/future suggests to me, above all, "clarity" of some kind, but in this romantic context, the idea of a polarity and the unity of opposites might be relevant. All three of these Runes are fairly "big" archetypal forces (to be fair, much of the Elder Futhark is that way), so the main overall takeaway I'd add is that factors and forces larger than your immediate context, possibly with a spiritual flavoring, seem to be most relevant to the question.

replies.title: Re: Rune help

Hi Jeff,

You might be interested in the way I usually divine with runes. Like your method, it works by rows of three runes (Urðr, Verðandi, Skuld), but I draw runes for three such rows, one under another, giving a 3x3 array of nine runes in all. In such a 3x3 array there are not three, but five, sequences of Urðr-Verðandi-Skuld, including the two diagonals. From five such sequences one can get a richer, or more nuanced, reading than from just one. (I use the Elder Fuþark, on wooden tiles, which I (or the querent) draw from a bag.)

replies.title: Re: Walking on the Moon

So you'd have to adapt the house tables for a much smaller world, move the site of the landing 238,000 miles off Earth (with modest but significant changes in planetary positions resulting from this). I'd love to see it, but it would take some work, and skills that most astrologers don't have.

replies.title: Re: Walking on the Moon
replies.content:
I thought about doing this as well, and worked out what I'd need to be able to do for it. I'm working on gathering the information and math skills for it, but I think it's going to take a while. I'd also like to note that if, as seems likely to me, 0 degrees Aries is determined by the position of the equinox on the Moon, and not Earth's, that the signs will be different, as the moon's axis does not tilt in the same direction as Earth's. Nor, for that matter, is it fixed: lunar precision takes around 18 years to complete a full cycle.

Then there's also the issue of that fact that most house systems depend in part on axial tilt; and the Moon's axial tilt is different from Earth's, being ~1.5 to our ~23.5. This means that the house tables will need to be completely recalculated.

comments.title: Gods
comments.content:
Hello JMG

This is something I can't understand. Why do we actually make sacrifices to the Gods?

I understand in the past, meat was very expensive (and sometimes it was a matter of being or not being for some family in the countryside) but now all I have to do is go to the store and I have grade A beef.

Likewise any alcohol and so on.

What do the Gods actually obtain from our sacrifices? Nowadays, wouldn't a better sacrifice for the Gods really be sincere prayer, meditation on their essence and living in , "in accordance with their commandments" ?

Nowadays our attention is one of the most valuable things we have (the amount of things that steal it is skyrocketing) so isn't our attention during prayer or meditation as valuable now as meat used to be?

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
In most traditional cultures, the sacrificed animal was cooked and shared out among the worshipers, and the alcohol wasn't wasted either. A sacrifice in ancient Greece, for example, was a barbecue in which one or more gods were honored guests, but everyone had a good time. In fact, in Greece you said the traditional words of the sacrificial ritual whenever you slaughtered an animal for meat.

In many modern faiths that do food offerings, what you do is you serve a tiny portion of each food the family is eating onto a small dish and put that in front of the altar. It's a way of inviting the gods to be present as welcome guests. The notion that sacrifice involves giving something up so that the god will benefit from it is purely a Judeo-Christian oddity, as far as I know.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
From what I understand, even in the OT, very few offerings were actually burnt completely. There were designated portions for the priests --an element of chance in selecting them, too-- they'd stick a long fork into the pot, and whatever they speared, that was their share. It comes up in the story of Samuel and Eli, because Samuel's no-good sons are grabbing the nice bits, and not doing it properly, so they don't get to succeed their father.

https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1%20Samuel%202:12-36&version=NIV

I don't know what scholars would say about that passage, but the fact that offerings are being boiled at all pretty strongly suggests they're being eaten at some point, rather than just burned up. At least part of the system of offerings in Judaism was basically a food tax that supported the priests, who did not have their own livestock. It wasn't just animals, if you read through the OT. It included things like grain (bread) as well. The "peace offering" seems to have been a shared meal. There *do* seem to have been offerings that were just straight-up burned and not eaten, but that's definitely not the whole picture.

comments.title: Divination vs Magic
comments.content:
Hello JMG, this seems like the more appropriate place for my follow up about divination.

I had always thought it was the use of a randomizing technique to select a symbol. I got this wrong: astrology’s coordinates are known far in advance. You mentioned a little bit about how divination is really a separate thing from magic.

replies.title: Re: Divination vs Magic
replies.content:
You're confusing one kind of divination with the whole field. Divination that involves one or more randomly drawn symbol is called sortilege. There are plenty of forms of divination that don't use randomness: think of astrology, numerology, and palmistry, just for starters.

Magic makes things happen. Divination figures out what's going to happen. They're related the same way as your hand and your eye are related.

comments.title: MOE Healer Grade
comments.content:
Hello there JMG

I hope you're well.

Some questions about the Modern Essenes Healer Grade material if I may.

For context, I've taken up the Essenes material full time, putting aside other practices and starting the Healing Breath meditation. This has meant halting my discursive meditation practice temporarily and postponing my plan to start working through the DMH. I had been doing the Essenes alongside my DM and Druidry Handbook studies, but realised I'd hugely benefit from doing one or the other. Essenes won for now.

Questions:

The Healer Grade material is curiously laid out. I've been reading and re-reading the sections on the "5 laws" (which changes to six and then back to five) and the seven emotional complexes. I'm scratching my head a bit, because I've either encountered a "flake filter" at one section (unlikely I think), or an error in the text or (most likely) I just don't get it yet.

Issues:
1. The Law of Intention is clearly laid out as an incomplete mirror of the Tree of Life material that follows in the Master Grade, but this isn't made explicit. It also incorporates the seven elements of the DMH et al.

The Seven Emotional Complexes (SEC) is explicitly laid out in association with the seven elements but also references Divine Father, Mother and Creator which seems to tie it back into the Tree of Life (TOL).

The Master Material, IIRC, then only deals with the Tree of Life structure and omits explicit reference to the seven elements (though I suppose its presence is still implied if I'm correct that the Law of Intention is laid out as the TOL). Is there a reason for this clear difference in the grades?

2. The SEC makes a lot of sense to me except in this area where it seems to reference the TOL:

-Unconditional Love (Spirit Above) "Spirit Above is equivalent to the Divine Father"
-Humility (Spirit Within) "Spirit Within is equivalent to the Divine Mother Incarnate in me"
-Forgiveness (Spirit Below) "Spirit Below is equivalent to the Divine Creator"

I find these assignations of SA, SW and SB confusing. Are they meant to be unrelated to the Law of Intention assignations for SA/SW/SB?

I did try this relation: matching the SEC SA/SW/SB to the same positions in the Law of Intention TOL (I hope I'm still making sense!) by reading "equivalent to DF/DM" as "Daughter of Divine Father/ Son of Divine Mother". But if that's right, I wondered if the SEC oughtn't have Divine Mother at Forgiveness - Spirit Below position and Divine Creator at Humility - Spirit Within, which would match Spheres 4/5/6 (written as points 2/3/4 in Law of Intention - REALLY hope I'm still making sense...) as well as balancing Mother/Father as opposites Above/Below. This was the error/flake filter bit.

If the above is quite wrong, can you give any hints as to how to read the inclusion of Divine Father/Mother/Creator in the SEC section? It's rather oblique.

Phew, sorry for the length!

JH

replies.title: Re: MOE Healer Grade
replies.content:
John Gilbert had many virtues but being well organized was not one of them. I have no idea what he meant by this particular set of correspondences -- no, he never explained them in my hearing. In all probability, knowing John, he wrote out the Healer material at one time, with one set of ideas in mind, and then wrote out the Master material months later, having forgotten what he had in mind at that earlier time and with a different set of ideas in mind.

Your best bet is to learn whatever you can from the material, and if you feel that you need a consistent system, do what John constantly did and revise it so it makes sense to you.

When I ask someone if I can pray for them, I realize that most people I might ask will assume I mean pray to the Christian god. In most cases I would not be willing to correct that assumption.

So when praying for someone who belongs to a faith I don't follow, to which god is it appropriate to pray? To a god with whom I have a relationship, which is probably not what the person meant to give me permission to do? Or to their god, with whom I don't have a praying relationship? To "the divine" in some general sense? Is there an etiquette around this?

Thank you,

Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Prayers
replies.content: I simply pray to my own gods, and ask them to work things out with the other person's god.

comments.title: Is anyone familiar with the "Right Use of Will" series?
comments.content:
Is anyone here familiar with the books of Ceanne Derohan?

She wrote an 8 book series, published from 1984 through 2007. Some have been revised as recently as 2021. The series is known by the title of the first book, “Right Use of Will.” I suppose metaphysical transpersonal depth psychology would be as good a term as any for the genre.

I have never seen anything about the author asking for donations or creating any organization. As far as I can tell, she simply is an author whose books are available from her publisher and the usual stores.

The books are presented as channeled messages from God, which are important to take in the order presented. “These books are a series and need to be read as such. They tell stories in a progression meant to surface things from the subconscious.” Throughout the books are admonitions to not just read the intellectual surface of the story, but to feel into how the symbolic events describe current hidden feelings.

I’ll provide a summary and some sample quotes below, in a couple of replies here.

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Is anyone familiar with the "Right Use of Will" series?
replies.content: Hmm. No, but then I don't tend to pay much attention to channeled books. There are so many of them, and most are pretty dreary.

replies.title: Summary
replies.content:
What follows is my own summary and any mistakes in this description of the contents are my own.

God wants people to know that God evolves and improves. We should not try to box in God, or anything else for that matter, with rigidly presumed definitions based on images from the past.

Because there was no experience or understanding at first, God the Father, primal Yang, the mostly intellectual Spirit, was unintentionally at first abusive and cruel to God the Mother, primal Yin, emotions that move to open the space for new creation to occur, the Will. From this original cause and subsequent mistakes in the often tragic cosmic soap opera of how creation began, a gap opened up between thought and feeling, desire and will. Between abstract knowledge, and the intuitive right understanding of wise specific application that includes the emotions - the right use of Will.

The gap leads to fragmentation of persons, including the Godhead, into fractured and traumatized shards embedded denial spirits that seek only a return to oblivion and death. These fragments sometimes wind up in other people. God’s most dangerous fragment is Lucifer, abstractly cool and cruel conqueror and scientist of manipulation, unmoved by suffering, who embodied as Hitler.

The cosmology is half of the books, including the emergence of various classes of spiritual beings, of the Earth as a pleasure garden later ruined, and of often magical prehistory of people in Pan, Lemuria, and Atlantis.

Whether men or women, people more comprised of Will essence have often been relentlessly oppressed by people with more concentrated Spirit essence. With intellectual smug superiority, the Spirit focused people wrap themselves in positions of power and condescendingly tell the cruelly subjugated Will focused people to just get over it already. Going somewhere else doesn’t help if the inner healing work remains undone. There are some scathing rebukes of the New Age “light brigade” that wants to abandon the earth to hell, while blissfully sailing off into paradise with their upper chakras only.

The other half of the books are a continuous call to action that now that God’s worked through these cosmic issues, it is necessary for people to reconnect with frozen, discarded, terrified and enraged emotions, by letting them move in the body in an appararent hybrid primal or somatic therapy. There are cautions to be sure the setting will not include an opportunity to harm or endanger self or others. Advice is repeated to learn what has happened but to not take outward action to confront abusers.

I have read a lot of channeled material. This material does seem to be unique. When I later came across the Kaballistic idea of creation being incomplete, with the negative side of the sephorah as dangerous "under construction" zones of the universe, I saw a few similarities, but far from a complete correspondence.

replies.title: Re: Summary
replies.content: So noted -- but it's clear she knows very little about Adolf Hitler.

replies.title: Re: Summary
replies.content: Hitler is only discussed a little bit, not a major theme. I tried to breeze through an overview of some of the topics. I might not have the right wording for what that particular book meant to say about him.

replies.title: Re: Summary
replies.content: BTW, enough. You've posted more than enough for anyone interested to have a look, and I prefer not to have these posts cluttered up with unsolicited book reviews -- or, for that matter, proselytization.
